security_audit.summary: All 46 static findings are false positives. The skill is legitimate Azure Service Bus documentation containing code examples in markdown. The backtick patterns are from markdown code fences, not shell execution. Environment variable access is for Azure configuration (standard practice). No cryptographic algorithms or malicious patterns present.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Block Detection (False Positive)
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' at 35 locations. These are markdown code fences (```typescript, ```bash), not actual shell commands. This is a documentation skill with legitimate code examples.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Code blocks are clearly marked with markdown fences. No shell commands are executed - these are documentation examples for the Azure Service Bus SDK.

low_findings.title: Environment Variable Access (False Positive)
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged 'env_access' at line 34. The skill uses process.env.SERVICEBUS_NAMESPACE for Azure configuration - this is a standard, secure practice for cloud credentials.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Environment variable access is for legitimate Azure configuration. No secrets are hardcoded - this follows Azure best practices.

low_findings.title: Text Pattern Misidentification (False Positive)
low_findings.description: Static analyzer incorrectly flagged 'weak cryptographic algorithm' and 'system reconnaissance' patterns. These are normal documentation terms ('Entra ID', 'receive modes') misidentified by pattern matching.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The skill is pure documentation about Azure Service Bus messaging. No cryptographic operations or system reconnaissance present.

content.user_title: Build Azure Service Bus Messaging Apps
content.value_statement: This skill provides code examples and patterns for implementing Azure Service Bus messaging in JavaScript and TypeScript applications. It helps developers quickly implement queues, topics, and message handling.
actual_capabilities: Send and receive messages using Azure Service Bus SDK
Implement queues and topics/subscriptions for pub-sub patterns
Handle message sessions for ordered processing
Manage dead-letter queues for failed messages
limitations: Does not provision Azure resources - requires pre-configured namespace
Does not handle application-level business logic
Does not provide monitoring or metrics
Requires valid Azure credentials (Entra ID or connection string)

use_cases.title: Backend Developer Building Event-Driven Systems
use_cases.description: Implement async communication between microservices using Azure Service Bus queues and topics.

use_cases.title: Cloud Architect Designing Messaging Patterns
use_cases.description: Create scalable pub-sub architectures with topics, subscriptions, and message sessions.

use_cases.title: DevOps Engineer Setting Up Integration
use_cases.description: Connect Azure services and external systems using Service Bus message routing.

prompt_templates.title: Send a message to a queue
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Azure Service Bus skill to show me how to send a JSON message to a queue named 'orders'.

prompt_templates.title: Receive messages with subscription
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to set up a message subscription receiver that processes messages from a topic subscription.

prompt_templates.title: Handle dead-letter messages
prompt_templates.prompt: How do I process messages from a dead-letter queue to handle failed messages?

prompt_templates.title: Implement message sessions
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to use message sessions to ensure ordered processing of related messages.

output_examples.output: Code example showing how to create a ServiceBusClient, create a sender, and send a message with proper body and contentType.
Code example showing receiver setup with subscribe method, processMessage handler, and error handling callback.
Code example showing how to create a receiver for the dead-letter subqueue and process messages from it.

best_practices: Use Entra ID (Azure AD) authentication instead of connection strings in production
Reuse ServiceBusClient instances across your application for connection pooling
Always close senders and receivers when done to free resources
Implement proper error handling with processError callbacks
Use message sessions when order matters within a group of messages
anti_patterns: Creating a new ServiceBusClient for each message (causes connection overhead)
Hardcoding connection strings in source code (use environment variables)
Not handling dead-letter messages (leads to lost data)

faq.question: What authentication methods does this skill support?
faq.answer: The skill shows both Entra ID (DefaultAzureCredential) and connection string authentication. Entra ID is recommended for production.

faq.question: Can I use this skill with Azure Event Hubs?
faq.answer: No, this skill is specifically for Azure Service Bus. Event Hubs uses a different SDK (@azure/event-hubs).

faq.question: How do I handle messages that fail processing?
faq.answer: Use the deadLetterMessage method to move failed messages to the dead-letter queue, then process them separately.

faq.question: What is the difference between peekLock and receiveAndDelete?
faq.answer: peekLock keeps messages hidden until completed or abandoned. receiveAndDelete removes messages immediately. Use peekLock for reliable processing.

faq.question: Can I send messages to multiple queues in one batch?
faq.answer: Yes, use createMessageBatch() to add multiple messages, then send them together with sendMessages(batch) for better performance.

faq.question: Do I need to install the Azure Identity package?
faq.answer: Yes, @azure/identity is required for Entra ID authentication. Install it alongside @azure/service-bus.
